content.text: Big-O notation is a mathematical shorthand for describing how the runtime or memory usage of an algorithm grows as the size of its input increases. Instead of measuring exact seconds or bytes, it captures the
of growth — whether performance degrades slowly, quickly, or catastrophically as data scales up.

Big-O notation uses the letter O followed by a mathematical expression in parentheses, such as O(n) or O(n²), to classify algorithms by how their resource requirements grow relative to input size n. The "O" stands for "order of," meaning it describes the order of magnitude of growth, not an exact count. It focuses on the dominant term (the fastest-growing part) and ignores constants and lower-order terms, because at large scales those details become irrelevant.
For example, if an algorithm takes exactly
operations for input of size n, its Big-O is O(n²) — the n² term dominates everything else when n is large.

Every program runs on finite hardware. As data grows — from hundreds to millions to billions of items — an algorithm that felt fast on small inputs can become completely unusable. Big-O gives developers a universal language to compare algorithms independent of hardware speed, programming language, or implementation details.

Without complexity analysis, choosing the "right" algorithm is guesswork. With it, a developer can confidently predict that a sorting algorithm taking O(n log n) will remain practical on a million items, while an O(n²) one will likely time out or fail entirely at that scale. This kind of reasoning becomes critical when building AI systems, databases, search engines, or any application that processes large volumes of data.

Think of algorithms as vehicles and input size as distance. An O(1) algorithm is a teleporter — distance doesn't matter. An O(n) algorithm is a bicycle — time grows directly with distance. An O(n²) algorithm is someone who has to run back to the start after every single step — doubling the distance quadruples the effort. Big-O measures how much harder work gets as the journey grows longer.

of an algorithm's growth. Specifically, f(n) = O(g(n)) if there exist constants c and n₀ such that f(n) ≤ c·g(n) for all n ≥ n₀. In plain terms: beyond some input size, the algorithm's actual work is always at most some constant multiple of g(n).
In everyday engineering conversation and in this tutorial, Big-O is used informally to mean the tight bound — the best description of how the algorithm actually behaves.
O(3n) simplifies to O(n). The constant factor doesn't change the growth pattern.
O(n² + n) simplifies to O(n²). At large n, the smaller term is negligible.
A single loop over n items is O(n). A nested loop — one inside another — is O(n²). A loop inside a loop inside a loop is O(n³).
Count how many times a function calls itself and how the problem shrinks each call. Binary search halves the problem each step — that's O(log n).
If an algorithm does O(n) work then O(n²) work, the total is O(n + n²) = O(n²).
If an algorithm does O(n) outer iterations and O(log n) work inside each, the total is O(n log n).
The complexity classes — from fastest to slowest###

The algorithm always takes the same amount of time regardless of input size. Looking up a value by index in an array or accessing a hash table entry are O(1) operations. No matter if the array has 10 or 10 million items, the lookup takes the same number of steps.

def get_first(arr):
    return arr[0]  # Always one step — O(1)

The algorithm halves (or otherwise reduces by a fixed fraction) the problem with each step. Binary search is the classic example: searching a sorted array of 1,000,000 items takes at most 20 comparisons because log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20. This is extremely efficient and often the target for searching and tree-traversal problems.

def binary_search(arr, target):
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == target: return mid
        elif arr[mid] < target: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1
    return -1  # O(log n) — problem halves every iteration

The algorithm visits every element once. Searching an unsorted list, summing all elements, or finding the maximum value all require looking at each item exactly once. Doubling the input doubles the time.

This is the complexity of efficient sorting algorithms like merge sort, quicksort (average case), and heapsort. It arises when a problem is split into n sub-problems each taking O(log n) work. For 1 million items, O(n log n) means roughly 20 million operations — very manageable.

def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    left = merge_sort(arr[:mid])   # Recurse — O(log n) depth
    right = merge_sort(arr[mid:])
    return merge(left, right)      # Merge — O(n) per level
# Total: O(n log n)

Nested loops each iterating over the full input produce quadratic growth. Bubble sort and insertion sort are O(n²). At 1,000 items this means 1,000,000 operations; at 10,000 items it becomes 100,000,000 operations. This becomes impractical quickly.

The work doubles with every additional input element. This is characteristic of naive recursive solutions to problems like generating all subsets of a set or the Fibonacci sequence without memoization. At n=30, that's over a billion operations. At n=50, it's astronomically large.

The absolute worst common class. Generating all permutations of n items is O(n!). At n=10, that's 3,628,800 operations. At n=20, it's 2.4 quintillion. Used in brute-force solutions to problems like the Travelling Salesman.
, but algorithms can have different complexities depending on the input. Knowing all three is important for production systems.
The most favorable input. Linear search finds the target at index 0 — O(1).
Expected behavior over all possible inputs. Linear search expects to check half the list — O(n/2) = O(n).
The least favorable input. Linear search's target is the last element or absent — O(n).

Quicksort illustrates why this matters: its average case is O(n log n), but its worst case (a sorted array with the first element always chosen as pivot) degrades to O(n²). Real-world implementations add randomization specifically to avoid this worst case.

Big-O applies equally to memory usage, not just time. An algorithm that creates a copy of its input uses O(n) space. One that creates a matrix of size n×n uses O(n²) space. Recursive algorithms use O(depth) space on the call stack.
Understanding space complexity is critical when working with large datasets. A time-efficient algorithm that requires O(n²) memory may be unusable if n is a million items.
Different data structures offer different complexities for the same operations. Choosing the right data structure is often as impactful as choosing the right algorithm.---
Sorting is one of the most analyzed areas in computer science because it appears in virtually every application. Here is how common sorting algorithms compare:---
Searching complexity depends heavily on whether data is sorted and what data structure it lives in.
Scans every element sequentially. Works on unsorted data. The only option if the data has no structure.
Requires a sorted array. Repeatedly halves the search range. Searching 1 billion sorted items takes at most 30 comparisons.
Computes the position directly from the key using a hash function. Near-instant for the average case, with O(n) in the degenerate worst case (all items collide).
Depth-First Search / Breadth-First Search on Graphs — O(V + E):
V = number of vertices, E = number of edges. The algorithm visits every vertex and traverses every edge exactly once.
Binary Search Tree — O(log n) average, O(n) worst:
The worst case occurs when the tree is completely unbalanced (like a linked list). Self-balancing trees (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee O(log n) worst case.
Greedy algorithms build solutions piece by piece, always choosing the locally optimal option. Their complexity depends on how many choices there are and how efficiently each choice is made.
Activity Selection / Interval Scheduling — O(n log n):
Sort intervals by end time (O(n log n)), then scan once to pick non-overlapping intervals (O(n)). The sort dominates.
With a binary heap (priority queue): O((V + E) log V)
With a Fibonacci heap: O(E + V log V)
Builds a priority queue of character frequencies and repeatedly merges the two lowest-frequency nodes. Each merge is O(log n) and there are n merges.
Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree — O(E log E):
Sort all edges by weight, then process each edge with a Union-Find structure. The sort dominates.
The key insight with greedy algorithms is that they are typically efficient in Big-O terms, but the challenge is
that the greedy choice always leads to a globally optimal solution — which is a correctness question, not a complexity question.
Recursion introduces a subtlety: each function call adds a stack frame, and the depth of the call stack determines space complexity. The
The Master Theorem is the standard tool for analyzing recursive algorithms. For a recurrence of the form T(n) = a·T(n/b) + f(n):
a = number of sub-problems per call
b = how much the problem shrinks per call
f(n) = work done outside recursive calls
If f(n) = O(n^(log_b a - ε)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a))
If f(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a)), then T(n) = Θ(n^(log_b a) · log n)
If f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b a + ε)), then T(n) = Θ(f(n))
Merge sort: T(n) = 2·T(n/2) + O(n). Here a=2, b=2, log_b(a)=1, f(n)=O(n). Case 2 applies → Θ(n log n).
Binary search: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1). Here a=1, b=2, log_b(a)=0, f(n)=O(1). Case 2 applies → Θ(log n).
Naive Fibonacci: T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1). The branching factor is ~2 and the depth is n → Θ(2ⁿ). Adding memoization reduces this to O(n) time and O(n) space.
Dynamic programming (DP) typically transforms an exponential-time recursive solution into a polynomial one by storing and reusing computed results. The complexity gain is dramatic.
DP table (n items × W weight capacity): O(n·W) time, O(n·W) space
Space-optimized DP (single row): O(n·W) time, O(W) space
DP table (m×n matrix): O(m·n) time, O(m·n) space
Three nested loops over V vertices: O(V³) time, O(V²) space
The general principle: DP trades exponential time for polynomial time by using O(states × work-per-state) computation. Each unique sub-problem state is solved exactly once.
Practical scale: what the numbers actually mean
Abstract Big-O classes become concrete when you map them to real input sizes and operation speeds. Assuming 10⁸ operations per second:
This table makes the danger of O(n²) and beyond viscerally clear. An algorithm that works fine in testing at n=100 can be completely unusable in production at n=10,000.
Ignoring the constant factor in practice.

Big-O ignores constants, but in practice they matter. An O(n log n) algorithm with a large constant can be slower than an O(n²) algorithm with a tiny constant for small n. Always profile for your actual input sizes.
Quicksort is O(n²) worst case, but its average case is O(n log n) and it is often faster in practice than merge sort due to better cache behavior. Worst-case analysis is conservative by design.
Accessing a hash table is O(1), but that single step might involve computing a hash function, cache misses, and pointer dereferencing — all of which have real costs. O(1) means
Missing the hidden O(n) inside library calls.
inside a loop produces O(n² log n) total complexity, not O(n log n). Every operation's complexity counts, including built-in functions.
Using O(n²) algorithms on large inputs.

A nested loop that compares every pair of items in a list of 100,000 elements produces 10 billion comparisons. This appears as a "stuck" or "frozen" program and is one of the most common real-world performance bugs.

An algorithm can be fast in time but create so much intermediate data that it exhausts memory and triggers garbage collection or swap — which destroys performance far more than the algorithm's time complexity suggested.
Analyze before optimizing. Write the straightforward version first, then measure where time is actually spent. Premature optimization wastes engineering effort on code paths that don't matter.
Always know the input size. The right algorithm depends on n. Insertion sort at O(n²) is perfectly correct and fast for 10 items. Merge sort at O(n log n) is necessary at 10 million items.
Choose data structures deliberately. Switching from a list to a set for membership tests often changes O(n) lookups to O(1) — one of the most impactful optimizations available.
Memoize recursive solutions. When a recursive function calls itself with the same arguments repeatedly, adding a cache converts exponential time to polynomial time with minimal code change.
Watch for O(n²) hiding in plain sight. String concatenation inside a loop in many languages creates a new string each iteration — O(n²) total. Use
Prefer O(n log n) sort algorithms over O(n²) for any input larger than a few hundred items. Use built-in sorts (Python's Timsort, Java's Arrays.sort) rather than implementing your own.

Big-O complexity analysis provides developers with a framework that goes far beyond raw benchmarking. It enables algorithm comparison before a single line is executed, scales predictions across many orders of magnitude of input size, and remains valid regardless of the specific hardware, programming language, or compiler in use.
An O(n log n) sort beats an O(n²) sort on every machine once n is large enough. You can reason about relative performance without running code.
Catching an O(n³) approach in design review is far cheaper than discovering it in production after the data has grown 100×.
'This runs in O(n log n)' communicates precisely to any engineer regardless of the team, stack, or context.
When profiling a slow system, knowing that the inner loop is O(n²) while everything else is O(n) immediately identifies the bottleneck without guesswork.
Big-O guides the choice between a list (O(n) lookup) and a hash map (O(1) average lookup) based on the application's access patterns.
Big-O is a powerful tool, but it has significant blind spots that cause real problems when engineers treat it as the only metric that matters.
Constants and cache effects matter in practice.

An algorithm that is O(n log n) with a large constant can run slower than an O(n²) algorithm with a tiny constant when n is small. More importantly, memory access patterns determine whether the CPU cache is used effectively. An O(n²) algorithm with sequential memory access (like insertion sort on a small array) often outperforms an O(n log n) algorithm with scattered memory access (like heapsort) in real measurements due to CPU cache warmth. Big-O says nothing about this.
Asymptotic analysis only applies for large n.

The "sufficiently large n" qualifier in the formal definition is crucial but often forgotten. For n < 50, insertion sort is often faster than quicksort in practice because the overhead of the divide-and-conquer recursion outweighs the asymptotic advantage. Python's built-in sort uses Timsort precisely because it performs O(n) on nearly-sorted data even though its worst-case is O(n log n).
Python's list.append() is O(1) amortized but O(n) in the worst case (when the internal array is reallocated). If your system has real-time latency requirements, amortized analysis is insufficient.

When an algorithm depends on multiple variables (m and n), collapsing them into a single n can lose critical information. O(m + n) for a graph with m edges and n vertices tells a different story than O(n²) when m is much smaller than n².

Big-O assumes a single sequential processor. An O(n²) algorithm parallelised across n GPUs runs in O(n) wall-clock time on a machine with enough processors, which is entirely invisible to classical Big-O analysis.

Complexity analysis is most valuable during algorithm selection, code review, and system design — particularly when dealing with potentially large inputs. It should be a first check, not a replacement for profiling.
Input size n can grow beyond a few hundred items and performance matters (any production system handling user data).
Choosing between two correct implementations of the same task (linear scan vs binary search, list vs set for membership tests).
Reviewing nested loops or recursive functions — these are the most common sources of O(n²) or worse complexity that sneaks into production code.
Designing data pipelines, batch processing jobs, or ML training loops where n can be millions of samples or tokens.
Input is always small and bounded (e.g., reading a config file with 20 keys). The readable O(n²) version is fine.
The bottleneck is I/O (network, disk) rather than computation. Optimising CPU complexity while the system waits seconds for a database query is premature optimisation.
Code is never reached in practice (dead code paths, error handlers). Profile first, then optimise what actually runs.
How to Analyse Complexity Step by Step
Analysing a piece of code for its time and space complexity follows a systematic process. Here is the procedure applied to progressively more complex examples.
Step 1: Identify the input variable n
Ask: what grows? Usually it is the length of the primary data structure — the list, string, graph, or matrix — but sometimes there are multiple variables. Make this explicit before continuing.
Step 2: Count operations in terms of n
Walk through the code and count operations as a function of n. Focus on loops and recursive calls — everything else (arithmetic, assignments, comparisons) is O(1) per execution.

Take the count from step 2 and simplify: drop constants, drop lower-order terms, and keep only the dominant term. If the code has sequential phases, add their complexities; if they are nested, multiply.

def process(arr):
    # Phase 1: sort — O(n log n)
    arr.sort()

    # Phase 2: scan once — O(n)
    total = 0
    for x in arr:
        total += x

    # Phase 3: binary search — O(log n)
    lo, hi = 0, len(arr) - 1
    while lo <= hi:
        mid = (lo + hi) // 2
        if arr[mid] == total: return mid
        elif arr[mid] < total: lo = mid + 1
        else: hi = mid - 1

    return -1
# Total: O(n log n) + O(n) + O(log n) = O(n log n)
# The sort dominates all other phases.
Step 4: Verify with the Master Theorem for recursion
For recursive algorithms, write the recurrence T(n) = a·T(n/b) + f(n) and apply the Master Theorem. This avoids manually tracing the recursion tree for every recursive function.

# Merge sort: T(n) = 2·T(n/2) + O(n)
# a=2, b=2, log_b(a) = log_2(2) = 1
# f(n) = O(n) = O(n^1)
# log_b(a) == 1, so Case 2 applies: T(n) = O(n log n)
def merge_sort(arr):
    if len(arr) <= 1: return arr
    mid = len(arr) // 2
    return merge(merge_sort(arr[:mid]), merge_sort(arr[mid:]))

# Quick sanity check: time it vs n
import time, random
for size in [1000, 10_000, 100_000]:
    data = [random.random() for _ in range(size)]
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    merge_sort(data)
    elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0
    print(f"n={size:>8}: {elapsed*1000:.2f}ms")  # Should scale as n*log(n)
Given a list of integers and a target sum, find any two numbers that add to the target. The naive approach uses a nested loop; the efficient approach uses a hash set.

from typing import Optional

def two_sum_naive(arr: list[int], target: int) -> Optional[tuple[int, int]]:
    """O(n²) time, O(1) space."""
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        for j in range(i + 1, len(arr)):  # nested loop → O(n²)
            if arr[i] + arr[j] == target:
                return arr[i], arr[j]
    return None

def two_sum_fast(arr: list[int], target: int) -> Optional[tuple[int, int]]:
    """O(n) time, O(n) space — trading memory for speed."""
    seen = set()
    for x in arr:                        # single loop → O(n)
        complement = target - x
        if complement in seen:           # O(1) lookup
            return x, complement
        seen.add(x)
    return None

# Both produce the same answers:
arr = [2, 7, 11, 15]
print(two_sum_naive(arr, 9))  # (2, 7)
print(two_sum_fast(arr, 9))   # (7, 2) — order may differ

# Scale comparison: n=100_000
import time, random
big = [random.randint(0, 200_000) for _ in range(100_000)]
target = big[12345] + big[67890]  # guaranteed to exist

for fn in [two_sum_fast]:           # skip naive (takes ~seconds)
    t0 = time.perf_counter()
    fn(big, target)
    print(f"{fn.__name__}: {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.2f}ms")
Word frequency counting is an O(n) operation using a hash map. Sorting the result to find the top-k words adds O(n log n) for sorting, or O(n log k) using a heap.

from collections import Counter
import heapq

def top_k_words(text: str, k: int = 10) -> list[tuple[str, int]]:
    """
    Find the k most common words.
    Time:  O(n) for counting + O(n log k) for heap = O(n log k)
    Space: O(v) where v = unique vocabulary size
    """
    words = text.lower().split()    # O(n)
    counts = Counter(words)         # O(n) — hash map

    # heapq.nlargest uses a min-heap of size k → O(n log k)
    # Much cheaper than O(n log n) full sort when k << n
    return heapq.nlargest(k, counts.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1])

# Example
text = "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox"
print(top_k_words(text, k=3))
# [('the', 3), ('fox', 2), ('quick', 1)]

# Full sort alternative (O(n log n))
def top_k_sort(text: str, k: int) -> list[tuple[str, int]]:
    counts = Counter(text.lower().split())
    return sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda kv: kv[1], reverse=True)[:k]

# For k << n, the heap version is faster; for k ≈ n, sort is equally fast.
Real-World Example: Token Frequency in an LLM Vocabulary

Large language models maintain a vocabulary of 50,000–100,000 tokens. When preprocessing text for training, you need to tokenise sentences and build statistics across billions of words. The choice between an O(n²) and an O(n) algorithm here is the difference between a job that takes days versus one that takes minutes.

from collections import defaultdict

def build_token_frequencies_fast(
    tokenised_corpus: list[list[int]],
) -> dict[int, int]:
    """
    Count how often each token ID appears across all sentences.
    Time:  O(total_tokens) where total_tokens = sum(len(s) for s in corpus)
    Space: O(vocab_size) for the frequency dictionary

    Production note: for corpora too large to fit in memory, stream the file
    and update the counter incrementally — same Big-O, no RAM constraint.
    """
    freq: dict[int, int] = defaultdict(int)
    for sentence in tokenised_corpus:    # O(num_sentences)
        for token_id in sentence:        # O(sentence_length)
            freq[token_id] += 1          # O(1) amortised
    return freq


def find_rare_tokens(
    freq: dict[int, int],
    threshold: int = 5,
) -> list[int]:
    """
    Return token IDs that appear fewer than `threshold` times.
    Rare tokens are candidates for merging or removal in tokeniser training.
    Time:  O(vocab_size)
    """
    return [token_id for token_id, count in freq.items() if count < threshold]


# Simulated corpus: 1M sentences of ~10 tokens each from a 32k vocabulary
import random
vocab_size = 32_000
num_sentences = 1_000_000
corpus = [
    [random.randint(0, vocab_size - 1) for _ in range(random.randint(5, 15))]
    for _ in range(num_sentences)  # do not run this inline — takes ~3s
]

freq = build_token_frequencies_fast(corpus)
rare = find_rare_tokens(freq, threshold=10)
print(f"Vocabulary: {vocab_size} tokens, rare (<10 occurrences): {len(rare)}")
# The entire operation scales linearly with corpus size — O(total_tokens).

The key lesson: using a hash map (defaultdict) makes each token lookup O(1) instead of O(vocab_size). The naive alternative — scanning the frequency list for each token — would produce an O(n·vocab_size) solution that becomes completely impractical at corpus scale.

Big-O analysis is the foundation of scalability reasoning. Understanding how algorithms compound as systems grow prevents the most common class of production performance failures: algorithms that work fine in development but degrade catastrophically under real load.

If a system processes n requests per second and each request involves an O(n) database scan over n stored records, the total work per second grows as O(n²). Doubling the user base quadruples database load. This quadratic relationship is one of the most common causes of services that appear fine at 10,000 users but collapse at 100,000.

The attention mechanism in Transformers has O(n²) time and memory complexity with respect to sequence length n. For a 4096-token context, attention requires ~16M operations. For a 128k-token context, that grows to ~16B operations — 1000× more. This is why efficient attention variants (FlashAttention, sliding window attention) exist: they reduce complexity to O(n) or O(n log n) while approximating the full attention result.
Several tools make complexity analysis practical in day-to-day development: from profilers that measure actual runtime to libraries that provide proven-optimal data structures and algorithms.

import cProfile, pstats, io

def profile(fn, *args, **kwargs):
    """Profile a function call and print the top-10 hottest lines."""
    pr = cProfile.Profile()
    pr.enable()
    result = fn(*args, **kwargs)
    pr.disable()

    stream = io.StringIO()
    ps = pstats.Stats(pr, stream=stream).sort_stats('cumulative')
    ps.print_stats(10)
    print(stream.getvalue())
    return result

# Usage: profile(my_function, arg1, arg2)
# Output shows call counts and cumulative time per function —
# an O(n²) function shows up as taking most of the time at large n.
Standard library data structures with known complexity
— O(1) append/pop from both ends (vs O(n) for list pop from front).
— O(log n) push/pop. Use for top-k problems and priority queues.
— O(log n) insert/delete into a sorted structure; pure Python but efficient.
— vectorised operations have the same Big-O as naive Python loops but with constant factors 10–100× smaller due to SIMD instructions and cache-friendly memory layouts.
Big-O is the most commonly used complexity measure, but it is not the only one. Different frameworks capture different aspects of algorithm behaviour.
Worst-case upper bound on growth rate
Ignores constants; worst-case may be rare
Harder to compute; requires proving lower bound
Average cost across a sequence of operations
Reflects real-world performance of dynamic arrays, hash tables
Misleading when latency spikes matter (real-time systems)
Actual runtime or memory on real hardware
Captures constants, cache effects, CPU branch prediction
Expected cost over a distribution of inputs
Requires defining the input distribution, which may not match reality

In practice, experienced engineers use Big-O for algorithm selection and design, amortised analysis for data structure selection, and benchmarking to validate that theoretical predictions match real-world behaviour before committing to a design.

Algorithmic complexity is directly exploitable as a security vulnerability. When an attacker can craft inputs that trigger worst-case behaviour in a server-side algorithm, they can cause a denial-of-service with minimal resources. This class of attack is known as an algorithmic complexity attack or ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service).

Early Python and Java implementations used predictable hash functions. An attacker who knows the hash function can craft a set of strings that all hash to the same bucket, turning O(1) lookup into O(n) lookup. Python mitigated this in 3.3 by randomising the hash seed at interpreter startup (PYTHONHASHSEED).
Many regular expression engines have worst-case exponential backtracking on carefully crafted inputs. A regex like

can take O(2ⁿ) time on an input string of n 'a' characters followed by a non-matching character. This has been exploited in production systems to take entire web servers offline with a single HTTP request.

import re, time

# Vulnerable regex: exponential backtracking
bad_pattern = re.compile(r'(a+)+$')

# Benign input — fast
start = time.perf_counter()
bad_pattern.match('a' * 20 + 'X')
print(f"n=20: {time.perf_counter()-start:.4f}s")  # ~0.0001s

# Malicious input — each extra 'a' roughly doubles the time
# DO NOT run with n>28 — it will hang
# start = time.perf_counter()
# bad_pattern.match('a' * 28 + 'X')
# print(f"n=28: {time.perf_counter()-start:.4f}s")  # ~several seconds

# Safe alternative: use re2 (linear-time regex)
# pip install google-re2
# import re2
# safe_pattern = re2.compile(r'(a+)+$')  # linear time guaranteed
(Google RE2, Rust's regex crate) when processing untrusted input. They guarantee O(n) for any pattern.
on any computation that processes user-controlled input. If a regex match takes more than 100ms, abort and return an error.
Validate input length before applying O(n²) or worse operations. Reject any input longer than a documented maximum.
, not exact speed. It answers: "how does this get slower as input grows?"
The complexity hierarchy from fastest to slowest is: O(1) → O(log n) → O(n) → O(n log n) → O(n²) → O(2ⁿ) → O(n!)
Drop constants and lower-order terms. O(3n² + 5n) = O(n²).
Best, average, and worst cases can differ significantly. Quicksort is O(n log n) on average but O(n²) in the worst case.
Space complexity matters as much as time complexity. Trading time for space (memoization, hash tables) is a fundamental DP and design pattern.
Dynamic programming converts O(2ⁿ) naive recursive solutions to O(n·states) by storing sub-problem results.
An O(n²) algorithm on inputs over tens of thousands of items is almost always a production bug waiting to be discovered.
Choosing the right data structure is often more impactful than choosing the right algorithm — switching from a list to a hash map can change O(n) lookups to O(1) across the entire program.
